questions.questionText: A patient with crushing retrosternal chest pain has ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. The infarct location is:
questions.explanation: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF localizes an inferior wall myocardial infarction, usually involving the right coronary artery. Anterior wall MI produces changes in V1-V4. Lateral wall MI affects I, aVL, V5, V6. Septal involvement is mainly V1-V2.
questions.source: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine, Ischemic Heart Disease

questions.questionText: The most important immediate investigation to confirm suspected infective endocarditis after blood cultures is:
questions.explanation: Echocardiography, especially transesophageal in many cases, is crucial for demonstrating vegetations, abscess, or valvular dysfunction in infective endocarditis after blood cultures are obtained. Chest X-ray is nonspecific. Cardiac enzymes are for myocardial injury. Holter monitoring is not diagnostic for endocarditis.
questions.source: Davidson’s Principles and Practice of Medicine, Infective Endocarditis

questions.questionText: A collapsing pulse is classically associated with:
questions.explanation: Aortic regurgitation produces a high-volume collapsing or water-hammer pulse due to rapid runoff of blood back into the ventricle during diastole. Mitral stenosis typically gives a low-volume pulse. Tamponade causes pulsus paradoxus. Constrictive pericarditis does not cause a classic collapsing pulse.
